company.name: Cursor
company.tagline: 24 months from a VS Code fork to a $29B valuation
company.summary: Cursor wasn't an overnight viral hit. It's a two-stage curve: 22 months of slow burn, then 13 months of vertical growth. Every valuation jump rode a narrative moment — and the narrative moments were not luck. They were the systematic byproduct of the founders treating themselves as media properties.

events.title: Anysphere founded
events.description: Four MIT undergrads (Truell / Sanger / Asif / Lunnemark) incorporate Anysphere. Initial direction: AI autocomplete for CAD software.

events.title: Pre-seed $400K
events.description: Earliest funding to begin development.

events.title: Pivot from CAD to coding
events.description: Realized CAD market was stagnant and that GitHub Copilot's plugin-on-VS-Code architecture was structurally limited. Decided to fork the entire VS Code instead of writing a plugin.

events.title: Cursor v0 launch
events.description: Public release of Cursor as a VS Code fork — an AI-native editor, not a plugin riding inside another IDE.

events.title: Seed $8M
events.description: Led by OpenAI Startup Fund. Angels include Nat Friedman (former GitHub CEO) and Arash Ferdowsi (Dropbox co-founder).

events.title: Cursor Tab (Copilot++)
events.description: First feature that's clearly better than GitHub Copilot. Becomes the core daily-driver delight.

events.title: Series A $60M / $400M
events.description: Led by a16z and Thrive Capital. TechCrunch exclusive — the first time Cursor uses an 'exclusive leak' format that becomes the template for every subsequent round.

events.title: 8-year-old codes w/ Cursor
events.description: Cloudflare VP Ricky Robinett tweets a video of his 8-year-old daughter building a chatbot in 45 minutes. ~2.7M views (cumulative). Boosted by Tobi Lutke, Karpathy, Lenny Rachitsky.

events.title: Lex Fridman #447
events.description: All four founders, 2h 38m. The crossover moment from dev-twitter darling to 'serious AI company' in the eyes of investors and non-dev tech audiences.

events.title: Composer default-on (Cursor 0.40)
events.description: Multi-file editing — Composer feature ships default-on with Cursor 0.40. Moves Cursor from 'better autocomplete' to 'AI that edits across your whole codebase'. (Note: the 'Composer model' name is later reused for Cursor's in-house code model in October 2025.)

events.title: Acquires Supermaven
events.description: Buys Jacob Jackson (former Tabnine founder + ex-OpenAI) and his team. One move = tech + talent + competitor removal + 'consolidator' narrative.

events.title: Series B $105M / $2.6B
events.description: Led by Thrive Capital. Valuation jumps 6.5x in four months — almost entirely from narrative repricing, not revenue.

events.title: $100M ARR
events.description: 22 months from public launch. The fastest SaaS company to reach this milestone on record.

events.title: Karpathy 'vibe coding' tweet
events.description: 4.5M+ views. Karpathy names Cursor Composer + Sonnet as the example. Cursor becomes the de facto referent for the term — later inducted into the Collins Dictionary.

events.title: 1M DAU · 14K enterprise
events.description: Crosses one million daily active users. Fast Company World's 50 Most Innovative Companies, ranked #26.

events.title: Series C $900M / $9.9B
events.description: Led by Thrive, with Accel, a16z, DST. $500M ARR disclosed simultaneously — funding announcement + revenue milestone fired together for compound coverage.

events.title: Pro pricing controversy
events.description: Pro tier usage caps trigger community backlash. The only meaningful public-relations dent in the entire arc — fully absorbed by subsequent growth.

events.title: Cursor 2.0 + own Composer model
events.description: Multi-agent architecture, up to 8 agents in parallel, 4x speed. The shift from 'application layer' to 'application + own model' that supports the next valuation tier.

events.title: Series D $2.3B / $29.3B
events.description: Co-led by Accel and Coatue. Strategic participation from Google and Nvidia.

events.title: $1B ARR
events.description: The fastest SaaS to ever reach $1B ARR — 24 months from $1M. Disclosed concurrently with the Series D announcement, the canonical bundled-milestone move.

events.title: $2B ARR
events.description: The doubling pace continues. ARR roughly 2x every two months across the 2025–2026 stretch.

events.title: SpaceX $60B option deal
events.description: SpaceX announces an option to acquire Cursor for $60B by year-end, or pay $10B for ongoing collaboration. (Musk merged SpaceX with xAI in February 2026; the announcing party and contracting entity is SpaceX.)

platforms.name: X (Twitter)
platforms.description: Where the dev community's attention actually lives. Cursor's founders kept their accounts active and in-character from 2022 through 2026 — a non-negotiable for the entire arc.
platforms.catalyst: Karpathy's 'vibe coding' tweet (Feb 6, 2025) — 4.5M views. Karpathy named Cursor Composer + Sonnet specifically. Cursor became the de facto referent for the new paradigm.
platforms.whenItWorks: When founders can post in real voice — product updates, mini-demos, technical takes — for 18+ months before anyone is watching
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If the team treats X as a corporate megaphone. Nothing kills dev-twitter credibility faster than press-release tone

platforms.name: YouTube
platforms.description: Where dev-twitter doesn't reach. Long-form video forces depth, which converts dev credibility into investor and exec credibility — the exact audiences who set Series B+ valuations.
platforms.catalyst: Lex Fridman #447 (Oct 6, 2024) — 2 hours 38 minutes with all four founders. Series B valuation 6.5×'d in the four months that followed.
platforms.whenItWorks: When founders have technical depth that survives 2.5 hours of expert questioning, AND the product has architectural decisions worth talking about
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't go on long-form podcasts to talk about a thin product. The format will exposes the gaps

platforms.name: Hacker News
platforms.description: HN's audience can't be fooled by marketing. Front-page placement on Cursor v0, Cursor Tab, Composer, and 2.0 functioned as 'serious devs are paying attention' proof — visible to investors and competing engineers alike.
platforms.catalyst: Cursor 2.0 launch (Oct 29, 2025) — front page, hundreds of comments. Two weeks later: $29.3B Series D. The HN signal was upstream of the valuation conversation.
platforms.whenItWorks: When your launch has genuine technical novelty — multi-file editing, in-house model, agentic flows. The HN crowd rewards substance
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't post incremental updates. HN punishes 'we shipped a small feature' framing

platforms.name: Reddit
platforms.description: Less an acquisition channel, more the place where existing users stick. r/cursor and r/programming threads compound the 'this is what serious devs are using' social proof for newcomers Googling reviews.
platforms.catalyst: No single moment. Slow-burn community emergence through 2024–2025. By the time Cursor hit 1M DAU, r/cursor existed as the de facto support and advocacy hub.
platforms.whenItWorks: When you have a critical-mass user base willing to evangelize. Don't seed; let it emerge
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If you try to astroturf, the community detects it within days. Reddit is 100% community-trust dependent

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.description: Mostly inert until the enterprise sales motion exists. Used by enterprise buyers to verify 'is this company real?' before scheduling a procurement call. Funding announcement amplifier — never the source.
platforms.catalyst: Series C / D announcements re-shared by execs and VCs. Each cycle shifted the perceived legitimacy ceiling for enterprise buyers.
platforms.whenItWorks: When you're already known and need to confirm legitimacy at the buying-committee level
platforms.whenItDoesnt: As an acquisition channel for product-led growth in dev tools — near zero ROI. Don't waste effort

platforms.name: Instagram
platforms.effort: Zero — and it's the right number
platforms.description: Cursor never built an Instagram presence. The audience-product fit is wrong: IG users aren't shopping for AI dev tools, and dev tool content doesn't perform on IG's visual-first format.
platforms.catalyst: Useful as the counter-example — proof that 'be on every platform' is wrong. Cursor's selectivity here is part of the discipline that produced the rest of the playbook.
platforms.whenItWorks: For consumer-facing or creator-tool products with strong visual demos. Lovable, Cluely could justify it. Cursor cannot
platforms.whenItDoesnt: B2B dev tools. Skip it without guilt
